draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners want tactile feedback tied to their running rhythm, not just visual updates
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — gesture-driven feedback vs passive visual display
facade.label: Haptic pulse on pace
facade.acceptImplies: Weather app should use device vibration/haptics synced to pace, cadence, or alerts; treat the runner's body as an input surface
facade.rejectImplies: Weather app should rely on visual/audio only; haptics are distraction or unnecessary in active contexts

evidence.hypothesis: Runners want tactile feedback tied to their running rhythm, not just visual updates
evidence.implication: Weather app should use device vibration/haptics synced to pace, cadence, or alerts; treat the runner's body as an input surface

facade.hypothesis: Runners want to see real-time pace data and current conditions side-by-side, not sequentially
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (paired metrics) vs narrative (single focal point)
facade.label: Split screen: pace + weather
facade.acceptImplies: Users value parallel information scanning — design with tiled layouts, dual panels, or split views rather than sequential drill-downs
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer one metric at a time — design with card stacks, full-screen focus modes, or sequential reveals that guide attention to what matters now

facade.hypothesis: A dark background with bright, luminous typography creates alertness and readability for runners checking pace/weather mid-run without feeling harsh or draining
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool + calm vs energetic
facade.label: Glowing text on dark
facade.acceptImplies: User wants high contrast, energizing visuals that feel modern and tech-forward; may prefer dark mode and neon/bright accent colors
facade.rejectImplies: User finds dark backgrounds with bright text either too aggressive or too digital/clinical for a running app; may prefer softer, lighter, or more organic UI treatment

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from friendly, chat-like language ("Ready to crush it?") rather than formal meteorological reporting ("Precipitation probability: 40%").
facade.label: Conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Playful personality builds motivation and rapport; users want encouragement, not just data. Continue exploring casual, reward-based voice.
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prefer clarity and authority; minimize personality. Shift toward clean, trustworthy, clinical voice without flourishes.

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer dark interfaces with reduced cognitive load during runs, prioritizing glanceable data over detailed information
facade.label: Dark mode, minimal text
facade.acceptImplies: Design should use dark backgrounds with large, sparse typography; prioritize at-a-glance metrics (pace, distance, time) over secondary details
facade.rejectImplies: Runners may need more readable light modes or prefer information-rich displays that show weather details, splits, and contextual data simultaneously

facade.hypothesis: For runners checking weather before/during a run, a single scrollable screen with all key metrics (temp, wind, rain, UV) stacked vertically is faster than tabbed navigation.
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure: single unified view vs. segmented tabs
facade.label: One-screen dashboard
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer vertical density and continuous scroll. Likely to reject multi-screen tab patterns. Suggests minimal navigation overhead matters more than categorical organization.
facade.rejectImplies: Users need information grouped by category or context. Suggests tabs, sidebars, or card-based filtering would feel more organized and less overwhelming than a monolithic feed.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners need real-time weather urgency conveyed through animation speed and intensity, not static numbers — wind gust strength should *feel* threatening or calm through motion itself
facade.axisTargeted: Animation pacing: subtle drift vs aggressive buffeting; transitions reveal data rather than display it
facade.label: Breathing wind speed
facade.acceptImplies: Weather alerts and conditions should use dynamic motion (wind particles, ripples, pulsing overlays) that metaphorically embodies the actual condition; severity maps to animation intensity and frequency
facade.rejectImplies: Weather is purely informational; static numbers, icons, and text lists are sufficient; runners don't need kinaesthetic or metaphorical motion to understand conditions

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
draft.html:

62 °F
Partly Cloudy

8 mph

Run Readiness

Good conditions. Light wind, mild temp. Go for it!
